coredata.dc:title: Sign-inversion of elastic anisotropy in a bent-core nematic liquid crystal doped with carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, R.K.
Turlapati, S.
Rao, N.V.S.
Ghosh, S.
coredata.dc:description:
In most bent-core nematic liquid crystals the bent elastic constant (K
33 ) is usually lower than splay elastic constant (K
11 ) owing to the presence of short range smectic-C like correlations in nematic phase. Thus the elastic anisotropy (K
33
− K
11 ) is usually negative in bent-core nematics unlike rod-like nematic liquid crystals where K
33 is always greater than K
11 . Here we report a short-core bent-shaped nematic liquid crystal whose negative elastic anisotropy was turned to positive by minute addition of carbon nanodots (C-dots). The experimental results demonstrate significant crossover of dielectric anisotropy and splay-bent elastic constant values at ~125°C. This effect is attributed to gradual formation of chain-like structures of C-dots as the mixture is cooled from isotropic temperature. Threshold voltage reduces significantly at high temperature region owing to higher local electric field and reduced viscosity. Dielectric relaxations of nano-sized clusters become faster in nano-dispersed liquid crystal.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Temperature dependence of (a) threshold voltage, (b) dielectric constant and (c) dielectric anisotropy.
Variation of dielectric permittivity with applied electric field at (a) 100°C, (b) 120°C and (c) 140°C.
Temperature dependence of (a) splay elastic constant (K 11), (b) bend elastic constant (K 33) and (c) elastic anisotropy (ΔK= K 33–K 11) for non-doped BCN and nanocomposites.
(a)Dielectric spectra at 115°C and (b) temperature dependence of dielectric strength (δε) and relaxation frequency (fR) of peak P1.

Molecular structure and phase transition temperatures (°C) of bent-core nematic (BCN) compound (6-2M-F). The nematic phase thermal range (ΔN °C) of compound is obtained from DSC thermograms recorded with heating (first row) and cooling (second row) cycles at 5°Cmin−1 and confirmed by polarized optical microscopy. The enthalpies(ΔH in kJmol−1) and entropies (ΔS) are presented in parentheses.

Difference in dielectric permittivity between non-doped and C-dot doped sample at low voltage before Freedericksz transition (planar orientation) (Δε1) and at very high voltage, well above Freedericksz transition, when all the LC molecules are aligned along the electric field (homeotropic orientation) (Δε2).

head.ce:title: Sign-inversion of elastic anisotropy in a bent-core nematic liquid crystal doped with carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Calcutta, 92 Acharya Prafulla Chandra Road, Kolkata 700009, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Assam University, Silchar 788011, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In most bent-core nematic liquid crystals the bent elastic constant (K
33 ) is usually lower than splay elastic constant (K
11 ) owing to the presence of short range smectic-C like correlations in nematic phase. Thus the elastic anisotropy (K
33
− K
11 ) is usually negative in bent-core nematics unlike rod-like nematic liquid crystals where K
33 is always greater than K
11 . Here we report a short-core bent-shaped nematic liquid crystal whose negative elastic anisotropy was turned to positive by minute addition of carbon nanodots (C-dots). The experimental results demonstrate significant crossover of dielectric anisotropy and splay-bent elastic constant values at ~125°C. This effect is attributed to gradual formation of chain-like structures of C-dots as the mixture is cooled from isotropic temperature. Threshold voltage reduces significantly at high temperature region owing to higher local electric field and reduced viscosity. Dielectric relaxations of nano-sized clusters become faster in nano-dispersed liquid crystal.

ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the elastic properties of liquid crystals (LCs) is particularly interesting for facilitating their application in modern technologies. The macroscopic properties such as threshold voltage (Vth), response time are determined by the nature of elastic interactions between liquid crystal molecules. Thus a number of theoretical and experimental investigations have been done so far to reveal the microscopic properties of nematic liquid crystal, generally the Frank elastic constants [1–4]. Recent studies suggest that these elastic constants are greatly influenced by nano-sized guest particles [5–8]. Frank elastic constants are expressed in terms of orientation distribution and direct correlation functions of nematic liquid crystals. Experimental results suggest that the nanoparticles with intrinsic dipole moment couple to the orientational order of the macroscopic system resulting alteration of the elastic constants [7]. Thus interaction of nanoparticles with LC molecules is of both fundamental and technological interest. Several macroscopic properties of host LC can be tuned by this method without rigorous synthesis.

Elastic constants of liquid crystals are greatly influenced by the molecular shape. In typical rod-like molecules the bend elastic constant (K
33 ) is always larger than splay (K
11 ) and twist (K
22 ) elastic constants except near nematic-smectic transition. Thus the bent-splay elastic anisotropy (ΔK=
K
33 -K
11 ) is always positive throughout the nematic phase. However, when the molecule has a certain curvature at its core, the elastic anisotropy has been observed to be negative [9–12]. In such systems, owing to presence of smectic C-like correlations among the bent-shaped molecules in nematic phase, bending deformation is favorable than all other deformations, hence serious reduction in bent elastic constant is observed. To further exploit this anomalous nature of elastic constants in bent-core systems it was mixed with rod-like liquid crystals [4,13]. In these mixtures K
33 decreases continuously with increasing concentration of bent-core molecules [13]. Such hybrid systems enhance the understanding of elastic interaction among differently shaped molecules. Another hybrid system can be formed effectively by inserting nano materials in bent-core LCs. As nanoparticles have proven track record of influencing elastic constants of calamitic liquid crystals it is also expected to exhibit similar behavior in bent-core system. However to the best of our knowledge no such studies are reported so far. In this study we have focused on the effect of these carbon nanomaterials on the elastic property of a bent-core nematic (BCN) liquid crystal. Carbon nano-dots (C-dots) of size ~5–6nm are blended with a four-ring BCN LC at a small percentage (0.1%). C-dots are spherical carbon nanomaterials with high photoluminescence properties [14]. These have shown good compatibility with rod-like liquid crystals by modifying several important physical properties such as spontaneous polarization, response time, threshold voltage, viscosity of the host LC [15–16]. The dipole moments of C-dots are thought to be coupled with the surrounding LC molecules thus influencing the observed variables. Significant modifications of elastic and dielectric properties are observed after nano-dispersion. The dielectric anisotropy (Δε), splay and bend elastic constants are initially lower than pristine liquid crystal while cooling from isotropic temperature but enhance significantly after certain crossover temperature. These results suggest self-assembly of C-dots in chain-like pattern. Moreover elastic anisotropy, i.e. difference between bend and splay elastic constant (K
33 -K
11 ), which is negative for non-doped BCN, turns out to be positive by minute addition of C-dots. Lower threshold voltages for Freedericksz transition and enhanced dielectric anisotropy are observed in nano-dispersed system. This study is particularly interesting since the nature of interaction of spherical nanoparticles with these atypical bent-shaped molecules has not been explored so far.

The BCN material used is a four-ring molecule with wide bend angle (~144°). The sample is denoted by 6-2M-F. Enantiotropic nematic phase with thermal range of ~31°C and ~80°C is observed in the heating and cooling cycles respectively. The synthesis and details characterization of this compound is reported elsewhere [17–18]. Because of its reduced bent and short core as compared to typical bent-core molecules, it stands at the border line of typical bent-core and rod-like molecules and possesses fascinating intermediate properties. The molecular structure and phase transition temperatures are given in Table 1 . All chemicals are procured from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Highly fluorescent carbon nanodots were synthesized by adopting controlled pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and D-norvaline (DN) at a high temperature above the melting point of CA (Tm =153°C). Citric acid, which serves to make the core (1mol) and D-norvaline (3mol) serves to passivate the surface of the carbogenic core were used. Dropwise aqueous (deionized water) saturated acidic solution of D-norvaline (3.5g, 30mmol) (maintaining the pH<1) was added to aqueous saturated solution of citric acid monohydrate (2.1g, 10mmol) with constant stirring in a beaker followed by evaporation of water at 120°C from the reaction mixture to obtain a colorless powder. The powder was taken in a silica crucible and subjected to atmospheric oxidation at 300°C for 30min. During the carbonization process evolution of gases and dark brown colored viscous liquid formation was observed in crucible to indicate the amide bond formation [22]. The dark brown colored product cooled to room temperature, dissolved in dry acetone followed by filtration to obtain the dark brown solution of carbogenic C-dots in filtrate, which then subjected to rotary evaporation to yield passivated C-dots.

C-dots have been dispersed at 0.1wt.% concentration in host LC sample 6-2M-F followed by adding 10mL of dichloromethane (DCM) to the mixture. The mixture is sonicated for 3h to promote homogeneous dispersion and afterwards stirred at an elevated temperature until the solvent is vaporized. Finally the BCN+ C-dot composites are filled in ‘planar’ cells (Instec Inc. of thickness 5μm and 5×5mm2 indium tin oxide coated area with antiparallel rubbing) by capillary action at temperature well above the nematic-isotropic transition temperature of host LC. The cell was placed in Mettler FP82HT hot stage attached to an FP90 temperature controller ((Mettler-Toledo AG, Switzerland) for precise temperature control (±0.1°C). For dielectric characterization, we used an impedance gain analyzer HP4192A with a frequency range 10Hz to 13MHz. For measurement of C-V characteristics a bias voltage has been employed i.e. DC voltage with a high frequency AC voltage (1V, 5kHz) superimposed to it for eliminating the ionic contribution. The applied voltage is sufficient to reorient the molecules. The bias voltage was ramped from 0 to 15V in 0.5V step. At low voltages below threshold LC molecules are aligned along the rubbing direction i.e. perpendicular to the applied electric field direction. The resultant capacitance and dielectric permittivity measured at this planar geometry are denoted as C┴ and ε┴. The parallel components (C║ and ε║) were measured by extrapolating the voltage dependency of capacitance in the high voltage range (10V–15V) in homeotropic condition where the molecules are oriented parallel to the electric field direction. The dielectric anisotropy is calculated as Δε=ε║- ε┴.

In Freedericksz transition study we have used electric field-induced splay-bend deformation geometry. First, the threshold voltage was determined optically by employing a 2mW HeNe laser coupled to an amplified photodiode (FLC electronics), while the sample was put in between polarizer and analyzer combination. Again, Vth was also obtained from CV curve and found in good agreement with optical threshold voltage. The temperature dependence of threshold voltages for non-doped LC and BCN+C-dot nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1(a). Vth reduces after nanodispersion at high temperatures. The dipole moment of the nanodots interacts with the dipole moments of surrounding bent-core molecules to yield higher local electric field which facilitates the reorientation of the LC molecules to give lower threshold voltage. In comprehending the high photoluminescence property of C-dots, it has been established that C-dots behave as electrical dipoles. Ghosh et al. experimentally demonstrated the existence of electrical dipole moment of C-dots by defocused emission dipole imaging [23]. At high temperature the rotational viscosity reduces and the effect is much more prominent.

Dielectric permittivity and anisotropy as a function of temperature are shown in Fig. 1(b–c). For non-doped LC and BCN+C-dot nanocomposites ε┴ and ε║ increases upon cooling owing to the enhanced order parameter (S) (Fig. 1(b)). However ε║ tends to decrease near nematic-crystal transition. In bent-core nematics often the molecules form nano-sized aggregation called ‘cybotactic clusters’ at lower temperature. The presence of cybotactic clusters in the BCN sample under study is demonstrated in our previous study [18]. In such clusters inter molecular separation along the transverse direction is shorter than in the longitudinal direction. Also the molecules favor head to tail arrangement within the smectic layers yielding an antiparallel alignment of the longitudinal component of the dipole moment hence reducing ε║. On the other hand transverse components of the dipole moments lie in the same direction thus enhancing ε┴ [19]. After nanodispersion the absolute values of ε┴ increase marginally from pristine BCN over temperature however atypical behavior is observed for ε║ (Fig. 1(b)). Upon cooling from isotropic phase ε║ of doped sample is initially slightly lower than non-doped LC but grows rapidly and crosses that of pristine BCN near 130°C. Dielectric anisotropy also resembles similar characteristics (Fig. 1(c)).

To put further insight to this observation we have measured difference of dielectric permittivity between non-doped and BCN+C-dot nanocomposites well before the onset of Freedericksz transition (Δε1) and at high voltage (Δε2) where almost all the molecules are reoriented along the field (Fig. 2(a)–(c)). The spherical C-dots are dielectrically isotropic and induce random disorder in the nematic medium. Thus when they are uniformly dispersed in the LC matrix without forming any anisotropic structure Δε2 should be less or equal to Δε1. This is the case for the temperature range 145°C–130°C (Fig. 2(c)). But as the nematic order increases upon cooling, the nanodots arrange themselves in cylindrical arrays along the nematic director to avoid or lower the elastic distortions [20] and minimize the excluded volume. During Freedericksz transition when electric field reorients the LC molecules, the director reorientation may induce mechanical torque and previously formed C-dot chains get deformed. Again at high field when all the LC molecules are reoriented, C-dots can self-assemble to form new chains and contribute significantly to the parallel component of dielectric permittivity. Thus Δε2 is expected to be greater than Δε1 in such case (Fig. 2 (a)–(b)). At a typical low temperature 110°C, Δε2–Δε1 is ~0.12 (Table 2 ) which is quite a significant increase for such minute (0.1wt.%) nano-dispersion and possible only if the nanodots form anisotropic structures.

To determine the splay elastic constant K
11 we employed the following formula,
(1)
K 11
=
ε o
Δε
V th
2
/
π 2
where εo is free space permittivity, Δε is the dielectric anisotropy and Vth is the threshold voltage.

Bend elastic constant K
33 is extracted following Uchida's approach [21] by fitting the CV curve well above Vth with the expression
(2)
C −
C ⊥
C ∥
−
C ⊥
= 1 −
2 π
1 + ξ
Vth V
∫ 0 1
1 + κ
x 2
1 + ξ
x 2
dx
where ξ =
ε ∥
ε ⊥
− 1 and κ=(K
33 /K
11 )- 1 . The variation of splay (K
11 ) and bend (K
33 ) elastic constants and elastic anisotropy (ΔK= K
33 –K
11 ) over temperature are shown in Fig. 3 . For pristine BCN the value of K
11 varies between ~7 and 10pN while K
33 is relatively small (~5–7pN). Both elastic constants increase upon cooling owing to the enhanced ordering in the nematic media, gets saturated and decrease to some extent near NCr transition. This softening of elastic constants below 110°C indicates the possibility of occurring twist-bend nematic phase and requires further analysis. The elastic anisotropy is negative for pristine bent-core sample as other bent-core nematics reported earlier [9–12].

After the addition of C-dot dopant/fillers, the elastic constant of the system behaves in the following manner. At first K
11 starts lower than that of non-doped BCN at high temperature range (145°C–125°C) but then rise over the non-doped values on further cooling (Fig. 3a). The bend elastic constant K
33 also shows similar behavior as K
11 (Fig. 3b). This anomalous trend in elastic constants also stipulates the formation of nanodot chain below 130°C. Such highly anisotropic structures formed by C-dots oppose the splay and bending deformations in the system thus enhancing the elastic constant values. Large increase in K
33 relative to K
11 can be observed in the doped system below the crossover temperature resulting in positive elastic anisotropic values (Fig. 3c).

We have studied dielectric relaxations before and after nano dispersion. A typical dielectric spectrum reveals two relaxation modes one at low frequency (P1) and the other at high frequency (P2) region (Fig. 4a). The low frequency peak (~1kHz) accounts for the polar organizations of the bent-core molecules whereas P2 (~1MHz) occurs due to ITO relaxation as it is independent of composition and temperature.

In BCN+C-dot nanocomposites P1 shifts to higher frequency side. Addition of nanodots increases the amorphous content of the material and hence loosens the close packing of pristine BCN molecules. The movements of the polar clusters in such environment speed up owing to increased available free volume. Dielectric strength of P1 also increases in nano dispersed system due to increments in dielectric permittivity and large conductivity contribution at low frequency region (Fig. 4b).

In summary, we have investigated the effect of doping C-dots on dielectric and elastic properties of a bent-core nematic liquid crystal. It has been observed that dielectric anisotropy and elastic constants are lower than non-doped at high temperature range but gradually increase on cooling and grow over the pristine values below certain crossover temperature. This effect suggests the formation of anisotropic chain-like pattern of nanodots at lower temperature region. The elastic anisotropy which is negative for non-doped BCN turns to high positive values below the crossover temperature. The dielectric relaxation is faster in doped system owing to reduced rotational viscosity caused by dilution of the system by adding nanodots. This fundamental study reveals the effect of pattern formation of nanodots in bent-core nematic medium and resultant change in elastic properties which has never been explored before. Further, the modulation of elastic properties of BCN by doping minute amount of spherical nanomaterial would facilitate the use of bent-core materials for achieving novel functional materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author SG is grateful to the Department of Science and Technology, India for supporting this work under INSPIRE Faculty Award (IFA-13, PH-60) scheme. We also thank Prof. J. Shamanna for developing the program for calculation of K 33.
